coredata.dc:description: A highly selective magnetic composite adsorbent composed of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was fabricated and applied for the extraction and quantification of three bisphenols in soft drink and milk samples. Magnetite nanoparticles incorporated into the MIP enabled the adsorbent to be isolated from the sample solution with a magnet. The extraction efficiency toward target bisphenols was enhanced by the selective recognition sites of the MIP layer and also the abundant adsorption sites in GQDs. In the optimum condition, the composite adsorbent coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) exhibited linearity in the range of 10.0–200.0 µg L−1. The limit of detection was 3.0 µg L−1 while the limit of quantification was 10.0 µg L−1. Applied to determine bisphenols in real samples, the proposed method achieved relative recoveries from 84.3 % to 94.9 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of less than 7 %. The proposed method is simple, rapid, efficient and accurate. The adsorbent can be reused for up to four extraction and desorption cycles, thus reducing the cost of analysis.

FESEM image (A) and TEM image (B) of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP; VSM curves (C) of NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4 (a) and GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP (b) with an inset showing the dispersed adsorbent and its isolation with a magnet; TGA curve (D) of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent; nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms are for GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP (E) and GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP adsorbents (F).

Optimization of MSPE conditions using the composite GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent: The charts show the effects of desorption solvent volume (A), adsorbent dosage (B), extraction time (C), desorption time (D), stirring rate (E), sample pH (F), amount of NaCl (G), and sample volume (H). BPF = bisphenol F, BPA = bisphenol A, BPB = bisphenol B.
Extraction recoveries of three bisphenols using different composite adsorbents. BPF = bisphenol F, BPA = bisphenol A, BPB = bisphenol B.

HPLC chromatograms of an energy drink I (a), a spiked energy drink after MSPE extraction using the composite GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent (b) and the standard solution of three bisphenols (c). BPF = bisphenol F, BPA = bisphenol A, BPB = bisphenol B.
The analytical performances of the composite GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent coupled with HPLC-DAD for the extraction and determination of three bisphenols.

head.ce:title: An adsorbent of molecularly imprinted polymer incorporating graphene quantum dots to extract and determine bisphenols in soft drinks and milk

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective magnetic composite adsorbent composed of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was fabricated and applied for the extraction and quantification of three bisphenols in soft drink and milk samples. Magnetite nanoparticles incorporated into the MIP enabled the adsorbent to be isolated from the sample solution with a magnet. The extraction efficiency toward target bisphenols was enhanced by the selective recognition sites of the MIP layer and also the abundant adsorption sites in GQDs. In the optimum condition, the composite adsorbent coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) exhibited linearity in the range of 10.0–200.0 µg L−1. The limit of detection was 3.0 µg L−1 while the limit of quantification was 10.0 µg L−1. Applied to determine bisphenols in real samples, the proposed method achieved relative recoveries from 84.3 % to 94.9 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of less than 7 %. The proposed method is simple, rapid, efficient and accurate. The adsorbent can be reused for up to four extraction and desorption cycles, thus reducing the cost of analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenols phenolic compounds used as a primary substrates in the industrial production of plastics. Bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol B (BPB) and bisphenol F (BPF), are widely used in the production of plastic containers and cans for food (Shaaban et al., 2022; Behzadi, 2023). When these containers are exposed to high temperatures for long enough, the bisphenols can contaminate the food (Chen et al., 2022). Although these substances are present in low concentrations, both direct exposure and gradual accumulation can pose health hazards that can affect the endocrine glands and reproductive system and lead to cancer (Yang et al., 2023). There are several regulatory requirements concerning the concentration of bisphenols in foodstuffs. The European Union (EU) has issued a specific migration limit (SML) of no more than 0.05 mg kg−1 for all foods that come into contact with bisphenol A in plastics (European Commission, 2018). Therefore, it is necessary to accurately determine the content of bisphenols in food products.

Bisphenols can be determined by fluorescence spectroscopy (Spagnuolo et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2014), UV–visible spectroscopy (Alomairy et al., 2023), capillary electrophoresis (Zhang et al., 2015), gas chromatography (GC) (Azzouz et al., 2016; Martín-Pozo et al., 2021), liquid chromatography (LC) (Kholová et al., 2023; Caballero-Casero and Rubio, 2021) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Sobhi et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2014; Birer et al., 2021). HPLC is an efficient technique which can be used for both the quantitative and qualitative analysis of bisphenols because of its high analytical sensitivity and accuracy. However, direct analysis of bisphenols in real samples is quite difficult due to their low concentration and significant matrix interferences. Therefore, sample preparation is necessary to increase the concentration of the analyte and eliminate matrix interferences. (Lian et al., 2020). Sample preparation typically involves extracting the analyte and bisphenols have been extracted from real samples by using solid-phase extraction (SPE) (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2022), liquid-phase extraction (Shishov et al., 2023), liquid-liquid extraction (Sas et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020), stir-bar sorptive extraction (Liu et al., 2020) and magnetic-solid phase extraction (MSPE) (Wang et al., 2023). MSPE enables the quick separation of the extractant from the matrix with a magnet, reusability and produces high enrichment factors (Tan et al., 2020; Du et al., 2020). In MSPE, the selection of the adsorbent material is important and the adsorbent material is usually modified with iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles to provide magnetic properties (Wang et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2021). Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is a material that has garnered considerable attention in various applications. It offers good selectivity and is easily prepared (Basak et al., 2022). The combination of magnetic material and MIP offers an improvement in the selectivity of the adsorbent toward target analytes. Fe3O4@SiO2-MIPs (Yuan et al., 2016) and MIMIPs-DES (Xie et al., 2015) have been reported for the analysis of bisphenols. Adsorption can also be improved by the use of carbonaceous materials, which have drawn a lot of interest in recent years. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a suitable carbonaceous material for the extraction of bisphenols due to their large active surface area. They also present oxygen functionalities and disperse well in water (Bunkoed et al., 2020). Low toxicity and good biocompatibility are further advantages of GQDs (Kurniawan et al., 2022).

In this work, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were functionalized with amine-modified silica (NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4) and used as a ferromagnetic support for an MIP adsorbent. The MIP adsorbent was constructed by sol-gel polymerization, utilizing a functional monomer and a cross-linker in the presence of BPA, BPB and BPF as template molecules. The adsorption of bisphenols was enhanced by incorporating GQDs into the polymer matrix. The fabricated GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was used to extract and enrich the bisphenols by MSPE. The combination of magnetic MIP and GQDs enables the adsorbent to adsorb bisphenols by specific binding via hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic and π-π interactions. The aim of the study is to develop a highly selective composite adsorbent to extract bisphenol (BPA, BPB and BPF) from complex food samples, including soft drinks and milk. The extracted bisphenols were determined by HPLC-DAD.

Bisphenol A (>99.0 %), bisphenol B (>98.0 %) and bisphenol F (>99.0 %) were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Citric acid monohydrate was from KemAus (Australia). 3-Aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES, 99.0 %) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99.0 %) were from Sigma Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Loba Chemie (Mumbai, India). Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6 H2O) was from QRec Chemical Co., Ltd. (Auckland, New Zealand). Iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4 H2O), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) and acetonitrile (HPLC grade) were from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Methanol, ethanol and acetic acid (glacial) were from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand).

Chromatographic analysis was performed on HPLC 1100 series equipped with a diode array detector (DAD) (Agilent Technologies, Germany). Chromatographic separation occurred on a reversed-phase Fortis C18 analytical column (5 µm, 4.6 × 150 mm I.D.) at a constant flow rate of 0.9 mL min−1. An isocratic elution was conducted using a mobile phase consisting of acetonitrile and ultrapure water (50:50 %, v/v). The wavelength of detection was set at 210 nm for the three bisphenols. The injection volume was 20 µL. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Apreo FEI, Netherlands) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM 2010, Japan) were used to investigate the morphology of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent. The chemical composition of the related materials for adsorbent fabrication was examined by FTIR spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). Adsorption-desorption nitrogen isotherms were plotted using a surface area analyzer (ASAP2460, Micromeritics, USA) to measure the surface area of the materials. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA8000, PerkinElmer, USA) was employed to study the thermal stability of the adsorbent. Magnetic property was measured with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) developed in-house.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of iron oxide functionalized amine-modified silica
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) were prepared following a previous work (Kliangsuwan et al., 2022). Firstly, 4.70 g of FeCl3·6 H2O and 1.70 g of FeCl2·4 H2O were weighed out and dissolved in 80.0 mL of DI water. With a gentle stirring under nitrogen, the solution was gradually heated up to 80 °C and held at that temperature while 10.0 mL of NH4OH (25 %, v/v) were added. The mixture was then refluxed for 1 h. During this step, the solution turned from an orange color to a dark solution with dispersed black particles. After cooling to ambient temperature, the Fe3O4 NPs were separated from the solution and extracted with a magnet applied externally. They were then washed with DI water and dried at 80 °C.

To functionalize the Fe3O4 NPs with NH2 groups, 100.0 mL of a mixture solution containing 50.0 mL of ethanol and 50.0 mL of DI water was prepared and transferred to a round-bottomed flask. Next, 0.50 g of the Fe3O4 NPs were dispersed in the solution and sonicated for 30 min. After that, 700 µL of APTES were introduced and the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 4 with 1.0 M acetic acid. The pH-adjusted mixture solution was stirred continuously for 3 h at 60 °C to obtain NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4 NPs, which were washed with DI water, dried at 60 °C for 8 h and kept in a desiccator until used.

GQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis using citric acid as a precursor (Bunkoed et al., 2020). Briefly, 2.0 g of citric acid were heated using a heating mantle in a round-bottomed flask. At 220 °C, white solid particles started to melt, turning into a liquid state with a pale yellow color. Separately, a 0.25 M NaOH solution was prepared in a volume of 100 mL and 1.5 mL of APTES were added with stirring at 40 °C. The pale yellow citric acid liquid was then added to the above solution with stirring for 30 min. The resulting solution was placed in a dialysis bag and continuously stirred for 12 h to separate the unreacted fraction. The obtained GQDs were stored at 4 °C until required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer embedded with graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The composite GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was prepared by sol-gel polymerization using three bisphenols as template molecules (Bunkoed et al., 2020; Kliangsuwan et al., 2022). Firstly, 4.0 mg of BPF, 4.6 mg of BPA and 4.8 mg of BPB were dissolved in 30.0 mL of methanol in an amber reagent glass bottle. Next, 360 μL of the functional monomer (APTES) were introduced into the mixture solution and stirred for 1 h at 400 rpm. Subsequently, 5.0 mL of GQDs and 30.0 mg of NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4 NPs were added and continuously stirred for 1 h at 400 rpm. After that, 1.60 mL of cross-linker (TEOS) and 1.0 mL of catalyst (NH4OH) were added to the solution, which was stirred for 16 h at 400 rpm to complete polymerization. The obtained product was then rinsed and sonicated in 40.0 mL of methanol for 10 min to remove the bisphenol templates. The synthesized GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP particles were collected with an external magnet. A composite adsorbent of non-imprinted polymer (GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP) was also prepared using the same synthesis method, except that no bisphenol templates were added. The fabrication of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP is displayed in Fig. 1A.

The MSPE procedure using the composite adsorbent is demonstrated in Fig. 1B. Firstly, 75.0 mg of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent were dispersed in 10.0 mL of DI water or sample solution with stirring at 1000 rpm for 20 min. The adsorbent was then separated with a magnet. The analytes were eluted from the adsorbent by sonication for 10 min in 3.0 mL of a mixture solution of acetonitrile and acetic acid (2.0 %, v/v). The eluted solution was evaporated at 50 °C using a rotatory evaporator. Then, 1.0 mL of HPLC mobile phase was used to dissolve the residue of bisphenols. The obtained solution was filtered through a syringe filter, and 20 μL were injected into the chromatographic system for analysis.

Milk and two types of soft drinks, sparkling water and energy drink, were selected. The soft drink samples were diluted with DI water (20 times) and extracted directly by MSPE without any pretreatment. Two different types of milk were chosen and pretreated as follows. First, 10.0 mL of milk sample were centrifuged for 10.0 min at 5000 rpm to remove fat. To remove proteins from defatted samples, 10.0 mL of acetonitrile were added and the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10.0 min. The supernatant was collected and evaporated to dryness and the residue was dissolved in 10.0 mL of DI water and diluted 20 times before extraction with MSPE (Cheng et al., 2024).

The chemical composition of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP was investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. The spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. S1a) presented an absorption peak at 585 cm−1 and an absorption band around 3409 cm−1, which were attributed to Fe-O and O-H stretching. The spectrum of NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4 (Fig. S1b) exhibited a peak at 1082 cm−1 from asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si. The small absorption peak at 1629 cm−1 was caused by bending vibrations of N-H in amino functional groups. The absorption peak at 3393 cm−1 revealed stretching vibrations of O-H and H-N. GQDs (Fig. S1c) produced characteristic peaks at 1128 and 1400 cm−1, which corresponded to stretching vibrations of C-O (alkoxy) and C-O (carboxy) functional groups. The sharp peak at 1578 cm−1 was due to CC stretching vibrations, whereas the strong absorption peak at approximately 3415 cm−1 was assigned to O-H stretching. The spectrum of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP (Fig. S1d) presented the characteristic spectral features of each component at the same wavenumbers as they appeared in their respective spectra. The features indicated stretching vibrations of Fe-O, Si-O-Si, O-H and H-N from NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4, and C-O and CC from GQDs. These results confirmed that the polymerization and fabrication of the composite GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent were successful.

The morphology of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP was observed by FESEM and TEM. In FESEM images, GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP presented as spheres with rough surfaces ( Fig. 2A), which could enhance the adsorption of bisphenols. Whereas the TEM image demonstrated dark and bright regions corresponding to the core-shell structure of the composite adsorbent (Fig. 2B).

The magnetic properties of the fabricated GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent were evaluated by VSM at room temperature. Both NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4 and GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP produced S-shaped hysteresis curves (Fig. 2C). The indicated saturation magnetization values were 66.10 and 15.05 emu g−1, respectively, confirming that both materials were superparamagnetic. Although GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP had a substantially lower magnetization value than NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4, the magnetic strength of the composite adsorbent was sufficient to enable separation with a magnet (the inset of Fig. 2C).

The thermal stability and mass ratio of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP were evaluated by TGA between 25 and 900 ℃. The TGA curve of the composite adsorbent (Fig. 2D) showed that weight loss was approximately 4.15 % from 25 to 150 ℃. This loss was due to the volatilization of chemically and physically adsorbed residual solvent and water. From 150–350 ℃, the weight loss was about 5.77 %, which represented the initial decomposition of functional groups on the adsorbent surface. From 350–600 °C, the weight loss was about 9.38 %, which was due to the destruction of the organic polymeric layer of the MIP adsorbent. Above 600 °C, the weight loss of 0.62 % was due to the loss of some organic substances on the surface of the SiO2-Fe3O4 layer. These results indicated the excellent thermal stability of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP below 150℃. The adsorbent could therefore be used for the extraction of bisphenols at room temperature (31±2 °C). The thermal stability of the developed GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was compared with other reported methods, including magnetic adsorbents that incorporate covalent organic frameworks (Zhao et al., 2024) and those that incorporate MIP (You et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2017). The results revealed that the developed adsorbent exhibited superior or comparable thermal stability, ranging from 25 to 500 °C, relative to the reported methods, alongside a notable improvement in thermal stability extending from 500 to 800 °C.

The surface area and porous structure of the fabricated adsorbent were calculated and identified from N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. Both the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP (Fig. 2E) and GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP adsorbents (Fig. 2F) exhibited type IV isotherms with hysteresis, according to the IUPAC classification. These results revealed that both were mesoporous materials. However, the specific surface area of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP (65.25 m2 g−1) was gretaer than that of GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP (60.49 m2 g−1). This difference was attributed to the recognition sites created by the removal of the bisphenol templates from the adsorbent. The successful synthesis of the composite Fe3O4-SiO2-NH2@GQDs@MIP adsorbent was confirmed, along with the presence of sufficient active sites for the adsorption of the three target analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the composite adsorbent fabrication and MSPE conditions
Ratio of template (TM) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL)
ce:section.ce:para: The mole ratio of TM to MN to CL was optimized as follows. The ratio of TM to MN was studied at the range of 1:15, 1:25 and 1:35, while the mole ratio of TM to CL was considered at 1:110, 1:120 and 1:130. The extraction of all three bisphenols increased with the increment of APTES ratio from 15 to 25 due to the abundance of monomers that could react with bisphenol template to form recognition sites. This provided more adsorption cavities for bisphenol adsorption after the template molecule was removed via hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic and π-π interactions, thereby improving extraction efficiency. However, at an APTES ratio of 35, extraction efficiency declined. Excess monomers can self-polymerize, disrupting polymer imprinting and limiting the formation of recognition sites, thus reducing the number of adsorption cavities. At the TEOS ratio of 130, extraction efficiency declined because the generated polymer became too rigid and binding with the bisphenol templates became more difficult, reducing the number of specific recognition sites available for bisphenol interaction. Similarly, at the TEOS ratio of 110 extraction efficiency decreases due to the generation of unstable polymer networks, which affected the stability of the adsorbent during the washing and extraction steps. Considering the results of these experiments (Fig. S2A), the ratio of TM to MN to CL of 1:25:120 was selected for the fabrication of adsorbent.

The volume of GQDs was studied from 0.0 to 20.0 mL. Extraction efficiency was improved when the volume of GQDs was increased to 5.0 mL. However, a slight drop in extraction efficiency was seen when the volume of GQDs was increased further (Fig. S2B). The initial increase in the volume of GQDs enhanced the adsorption of bisphenol, but an excess volume of GQDs could block the imprinted recognition sites. Hence, the optimum volume of GQDs was 5.0 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type and volume of desorption solvent
ce:section.ce:para: Various organic solvents were selected based on the polarity of bisphenols. Methanol, acetone, acetonitrile and a solvent acidified with acetic acid were tested, as they have similar polality with bisphenols where they could efficiently desorb the bisphenols from the adsorbent (Jiang et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2023). A mixture of acetonitrile and 2.0 % v/v of acetic acid provided the highest extraction efficiency (Fig. S2C) and was therefore selected for further experiments. Perhaps this mixture could better disrupt the interaction between the adsorbent and the analytes, facilitating the elution of bisphenol from the adsorbent. The volume of this desorption solvent was then optimized between 2.0 and 6.0 mL. The best elution performance was obtained at a desorption volume of 3.0 mL ( Fig. 3A), which was selected as the optimum elution condition.

In the MSPE procedure, the dosage of adsorbent is a crucial parameter that influences extraction efficiency. The effect of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP dosage was evaluated between 25.0 and 200.0 mg (Fig. 3B). Extraction efficiency gradually increased with increments from 25.0 to 75.0 mg due to the increase in the number of active sites for the adsorption of bisphenols. There was no significant improvement in extraction efficiency at adsorbent dosages above 75.0 mg. Therefore, 75.0 mg of adsorbent was considered the optimum condition.

Extraction time was investigated between 10.0 and 50.0 min. Extraction efficiency significantly improved initially, and reached a plateau at 20.0 min (Fig. 3C). Beyond 20.0 min, there was no improvement in extraction efficiency. The shortest extraction time that produced the highest extraction of all three bisphenols was 20.0 min, and was the extraction time chosen for further experiments.

Desorption time could affect the elution of the adsorbed bisphenols. The effect of desorption time was studied from 5.0 to 40.0 min with ultrasonication. From 5.0–10.0 min, the extraction efficiency of the three bisphenols gradually increased (Fig. 3D). After 10.0 min, a significant decrease in extraction efficiency was seen. Prolonged desorption time may have caused the analytes to be reabsorbed onto the adsorbent. Therefore, desorption time was set at 10.0 min as the optimal condition.

During extraction, the mass transfer of analytes from the solution to the adsorbent can be accelerated by increasing the stirring speed. The effect of stirring speed here was explored at 500, 750, 1000, 1250 and 1500 rpm. The best extraction efficiency was achieved when a stirring speed of 1000 rpm was employed. At stirring speeds above or below 1000 rpm, extraction efficiency was low (Fig. 3E). At lower stirring speeds, the mass transfer of bisphenols was low, while at higher stirring speeds, air bubbles were produced, reducing the contact time between bisphenols and the adsorbent. Consequently, the optimal stirring speed was 1000 rpm.

Sample pH played a crucial role in the adsorption of bisphenols because, changes in pH could alter the form of the analytes. Consequently, the interaction between the analytes and the adsorbent as well as the stability of the adsorbent could be affected. Sample solution pH was adjusted in the range of 3.0–11.0. The extraction of all the target bisphenols reached a peak and remained relatively constant between pH 3.0 and 7.0. At pH > 7.0, particularly between 9.0 and 11.0, a sharp decrease in extraction was observed (Fig. 3F). In the alkaline condition, the bisphenols, which have a pKa value of around 9–10, were deprotonated into anionic species, and binding with the adsorbent was limited (Ma et al., 2024). In addition, the MIP surface layer could decompose in the alkaline condition. As the bisphenols existed in the molecular form when the solution was acidic or neutral, they interacted with the adsorbent via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Considering that the real samples tested normally present pH values in the range of 3.0–7.0, the remaining experiments were performed without any pH adjustment.

The effect of ionic strength was examined by adding NaCl at concentrations ranging from 0.0 % to 15.0 % w/v to the samples. As shown in Fig. 3G, recoveries increased with increasing salt concentration from 0.0 % to 3.0 % w/v. The salting-out effect promoted the mass transfer of bisphenols to the adsorbent by reducing the solubility of bisphenols in the aqueous solution. On the other hand, at salt concentrations above 3.0 %, extraction efficiency decreased due to the increased viscosity of the aqueous solution, which is the other side of the salting-out effect that reduced the mass transfer of analytes in the system (Peng et al., 2020). Therefore, 3.0 % w/v of NaCl was selected as the optimum salt concentration.

To obtain a high enrichment factor, that enhances the extraction of target analytes at low concentration levels, sample volume was varied from 5.0 to 20.0 mL. The extraction of all three target bisphenols was satisfactory when the sample volume was 10.0 mL (Fig. 3H). Above 10.0 mL, extraction efficiency significantly decreased, perhaps due to the reduced contact between the increasing solution volume and the unchanged adsorbent surface area. A sample volume of 10.0 mL produced a higher enrichment factor than 5.0 mL and still provided good adsorption performances. A summary of the optimal conditions can be seen in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different types of composite adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: Four different composite adsorbents were fabricated and employed to extract and enrich BPF, BPA and BPB by MSPE in the optimal condition. The GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent exhibited the best extraction efficiency for all target bisphenols ( Fig. 4). The performance of this adsorbent was attributed the large number of absorption sites produced by the specific imprinted cavities of MIP and the GQDs. The presence of these two materials produced specific binding between the adsorbent and target analytes and enhanced the adsorption of bisphenols via hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interaction and π-π interactions (Fig. S3). The NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent showed better extraction efficiency than the NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP and GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP adsorbents, due to the presence of specific binding sites for bisphenols. The reduction in extraction efficiency in the absence of GQDs was attributed to the presence of fewer adsorption sites on the adsorbent. The NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@NIP produced the lowest extraction recoveries since it had no specific binding sites which limited the adsorption of the target bisphenols to non-specific binding. Based on these results, the composite GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was applied for the extraction of bisphenols in sparkling water, energy drink and milk samples.

To better understand the adsorption of bisphenols on the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent, the pseudo-first order (PFO) and pseudo-second order (PSO) adsorption kinetics models were applied. A 10.0 mL sample solution containing 10.0 μg L−1 of bisphenols was extracted for different times on 75.0 mg of the adsorbent. For the PFO model, ln(qe-qt) was plotted against t, while for the PSO model, t/qt was ploted against t. The experimental data were fitted to the kinetic models as follows:
(1) PFO equation; ln (qe - qt) = lnqe – k1t
(2) PSO equation; t/qt = 1/(k2qe 2) + t/qe

In the above equations, qe and qt are the adsorption capacities (mg g−1) of bisphenols on the adsorbent at equilibrium and time t, respectively. k1 (min−1) and k2 (g mg−1 min−1) are the rate constants of PFO and PSO, repectively.

The coefficients of determination (R2) were used to compare the suitability of the adsorption kinetics models: the higher R2 value suggesting a stronger correlation between the theoretical values and the experimental data (Yang et al., 2023). The PSO model showed better consistent kinetics data than the PFO model, with an R2 greater than 0.99 for all three bisphenols (Table S2). These results indicated that the adsorption of the target bisphenols on the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was a chemical adsorption process.

In the analysis of real samples, the complexity of the matrix in soft drinks and milk samples could influence the analytical results. The matrix effect (ME) was evaluated by using the following equation: ME (%) = ((Slope of bisphenols calibration curve in spiked soft drinks and milk samples divided by slope of bisphenols calibration curve in spiked solvent)-1) × 100 (Liu et al., 2023). Both calibration curves were prepared at the same concentration levels. At a dilution factor of 20 times for all samples, the ME ranged from −11.9 % to −5.7 % (Table S3). Since the acceptable range for suppression or enhancement is considered 0–20 % (Rajski et al., 2013), the indicated matrix effects were soft or negligible. Even so, to ensure certainty, subsequent experiments used matrix-matched calibration curves of diluted samples for the quantitative analysis of bisphenols in real soft drinks and milk samples.

The developed GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was employed to validate the performance of the developed method in the optimum MSPE condition. BPF, BPA and BPB were extracted and enriched from spiked beverage samples at different concentrations before being quantified by HPLC-DAD. To evaluate the linearity of response, peak areas of response signals were plotted against spike concentration. A linear working range was achieved from 10.0 to 200.0 μg L−1 for all target bisphenols with R2 values greater than 0.99. Based on signal to noise ratios of 3 and 10, limits of detection (LODs) and limits of quantification (LOQs) were 3.0 μg L−1and 10.0 μg L−1, respectively. Repeatability was assessed in terms of relative standard deviations (RSDs) by conducting inter-day (n=6) and intra-day (n=6) experiments. The inter-day precision was in the range of 3.7–4.5 %, while the intra-day precision ranged from 3.3 % to 4.0 %. The results confirmed that the developed method could be effectively applied for the extraction and determination of bisphenols, even at low concentration levels, with satisfactory precision. The enrichment factor (EF) of the developed method was calculated by using the equation; EF = CFinal/CInitial, where CFinal is the concentration of target bisphenols from the extraction phase calculated from the linear working curves and CInitial is the initial concentration of target bisphenols present in the sample. In this work, the sample solution volume 10.0 mL and the concentration of spiked samples was 10.0 μg L−1. The EF was calculated to be 9.1 for BPF and BPA and 9.2 for BPB. The results were summarized in Table 1.

To verify the practicability of the developed method, the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was utilized to extract sparkling water, energy drink and milk samples ( Table 2). Bisphenol F was found in milk, sparkling water and energy drink samples. Bisphenol A and bisphenol B were not detected in any of the real samples or otherwise were below the detection limits. The accuracy of the developed method was determined in terms of relative recovery (RR) by spiking samples with bisphenol standards at three concentration levels (10.0, 20.0 and 50.0 μg L−1). The RR was calculated using the following equation; RR (%) = (CF-CR)/CS
× 100, where CF and CR are the concentrations of bisphenols measured in spiked and non-spiked samples, respectively, and Cs is the concentration of the spiked standard. In accordance with the results obtained in Section 3.5, employing matrix-matched calibration, the RRs of bisphenols at three varying concentrations were in the range of 84.3–94.9 % with RSDs less than 7 %. The results revealed that the developed method could be applied to determine bisphenols in real samples with good accuracy and precision, and was satisfactory for the extraction and quantification of residual bisphenols in soft drinks and milk samples. Representative HPLC chromatograms of a beverage sample, a spiked beverage sample obtained after extraction, and the standard solutions of three bisphenols are shown in Fig. 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and reusability of the developed adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the developed GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was investigated based on the RSDs value. Six different lots of the MIP adsorbent were fabricated in the same conditions and applied for the extraction of the three target bisphenols. The RSDs obtained from the average recoveries were lower than 4.0 % (Fig. S4), revealing that the fabricated adsorbent had good reproducibility.

The reusability of the GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was studied by repeating the MSPE procedure. Firstly, BPF, BPA and BPB were extracted and enriched using the same developed adsorbent for six consecutive cycles. Between each cycle, the developed adsorbent was washed using 3.0 mL of a mixture of acetonitrile and acetic acid (2.0 %, v/v) and 3.0 mL of DI water. The carryover effect was verified by injecting the washing solution into the HPLC-DAD system. As presented in Fig. S5, the MIP adsorbent produced recoveries greater than 80 % for four cycles. Extraction recoveries decreased when the adsorbent was used for more than four cycles because the specific binding cavities of the MIP adsorbent were damaged during the extraction, desorption, and washing processes. The damaged adsorbent presented fewer active sites to interact with target molecules. Based on the abovementioned results, the developed MIP adsorbent could be effectively used for four cycles, saving time in preparation and reducing the consumption of chemical reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with commercial adsorbent and other methods for bisphenols analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction performance of the developed GQDs@NH2-SiO2-Fe3O4@MIP adsorbent was compared with an octadecyl (C18) modified silica adsorbent (Fig. S6). The adsorbents both produced extraction recoveries higher than 80 %. However, the extraction recoveries of the proposed adsorbent were slightly higher since it offers a greater number of adsorption. The developed MIP adsorbent is convenient to use and does not suffer from the backpressure typical of commercial packed SPE cartridges. Furthermore, the MIP adsorbent does not require the use of an SPE manifold system. The performance of the proposed method was compared with those of other reported methods for the analysis of bisphenols in foodstuffs ( Table 3). In terms of analytical parameters, the performances of the proposed method were comparable. The developed method is also simpler, easier to operate, and highly selective. These results suggested that the developed method is practical and can be used for the analysis of bisphenols in real-world samples.

Amine-modified silica-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles and GQDs were embedded in MIP to create an adsorbent for the magnetic solid-phase extraction and enrichment of three bisphenols in soft drink and milk. The developed adsorbent was integrated with HPLC-DAD to extract and quantify trace-level bisphenols. The specificity of the recognition sites imprinted in the MIP layer promoted the selectivity of the adsorbent toward the analytes. GQDs increased the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent by presenting additional adsorption sites. The relative recoveries of three bisphenols from complex real samples demonstrated the selectivity and efficiency of the developed method. The simplicity, rapidity and reusability of the developed adsorbent minimizes the use of chemicals and reagents. Furthermore, the proposed adsorbent exhibits good reproducibility and could be used to extract other organic contaminants in various samples with the appropriate template modification.
